narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68% chance that you would not pass—well above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the three biggest influences pushing the model toward this concerning prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your family income bracket. Most significantly, your placement in the bottom decile for Year 3 grades strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure to the model, as successful students typically rank around the 6th decile there. Similarly, your bottom-decile Year 1 performance reinforced that concern, standing out well below the 6th-decile average among those who passed the bar. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward success, aligning with the majority (about 45%) of passers from similar backgrounds, though it wasn't enough to offset the academic flags. Compared to admitted students, your LSAT score of 32 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 also trail the averages of 37.27 and 3.24 seen among bar passers, contributing to the overall picture but ranking lower in influence for your specific prediction. Your choice of full-time enrollment matches the norm for 93% of successful students, which didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential or hard work—it's the model highlighting where your profile statistically diverges from those who typically succeed on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program that demonstrates upward momentum. Building a stronger narrative around your undergraduate performance, perhaps with explanations or recent achievements, could also help in reapplying or appealing. Many students in similar positions have improved their odds by targeting these areas—keep pushing, as your upper-middle background shows you're already in a supportive position to invest in that growth.
